query:
What are some negative/positive aspects from globalization in regard to developed countries?

I have read many articles on how globalization is helping developing countries, as more and more companies from developed countries have been moving their production oversees. However, as hard as I tried, I couldn't find a lot of articles talking about the opposite site of the story - How globalization affects developed countries.

Most of the articles I found were focused on the fact that moving production oversees will increase the unemployment and decrease the GDP. However, are there any other aspects (positive or negative)? Would love to learn more about this topic and if you have a good article that dives into this I would be really grateful if you can provide me with a link to it.
query_intent_analysis: The user is seeking a comprehensive understanding of the positive and negative impacts of globalization on developed countries. The core question revolves around identifying various aspects, beyond just unemployment and GDP, that are influenced by globalization in developed nations. The foundational cognitive framework required here is a multi-perspective economic and social analysis, which includes both qualitative and quantitative data.

  <node id="V1">Globalization leads to companies moving production overseas.</node>
  <node id="V2">Moving production overseas increases unemployment in developed countries.</node>
  <node id="V3">Moving production overseas decreases GDP in developed countries.</node>
</known_nodes>
<known_edges>
  <edge id="E1" source="V1" target="V2">Companies moving production overseas causes an increase in unemployment.</edge>
  <edge id="E2" source="V1" target="V3">Companies moving production overseas causes a decrease in GDP.</edge>
</known_edges>

</missing_nodes>

  <edge id="E4" source="V1" target="V5">Mechanisms or causal links leading to positive aspects of globalization on developed countries.</edge>
</missing_edges>

The known sub-graph provides a clear starting point with the movement of production overseas and its direct impacts on unemployment and GDP. The missing sub-graph identifies the need for additional nodes and edges to cover other aspects, both positive and negative. This structure suggests a [Divergent Graphs] topology, as the logical graph will share the same starting node (globalization and the movement of production) but will have multiple independent paths to explore different aspects (positive and negative impacts). query:
What is social science and discussion about Type II error preference?

Today, my senior lecturer in economics and finance class teaching about laws impact companies' operation told us one sentence "Also keep in mind that in (at least social) science, we care (try to avoid) about Type II (making a claim on some new finding while it is actually not true), more than Type I error (rejecting some new findings while it is actually true). "

From this link, the definition of social science is: "Social science is, in its broadest sense, the study of society and the manner in which people behave and influence the world around us."

Based on this definition, why economics relating to social science, and what is other than social science
query_intent_analysis: The user is seeking to understand the relationship between social science and economics, as well as the preference for avoiding Type II errors in social science. The core question involves understanding the definition of social science, how it relates to economics, and why there is a preference for avoiding Type II errors in this context. The foundational cognitive framework required is a combination of definitional clarity, logical reasoning, and an understanding of statistical error types.
logical_graph_construction:
<known_nodes>
  <node id="V1">Definition of social science: the study of society and the manner in which people behave and influence the world around us.</node>
  <node id="V2">Economics is a field that studies the production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services.</node>
  <node id="V3">Type II error: failing to reject a false null hypothesis (claiming a new finding is true when it is not).</node>
  <node id="V4">Type I error: rejecting a true null hypothesis (rejecting a new finding when it is actually true).</node>
  <node id="V5">In social science, there is a preference for avoiding Type II errors over Type I errors.</node>
</known_nodes>
<known_edges>
  <edge id="E1" source="V1" target="V2">Economics is a part of social science because it studies how people and societies produce, distribute, and consume goods and services, which influences behavior and societal structures.</edge>
  <edge id="E2" source="V3" target="V5">Avoiding Type II errors is preferred in social science because it is more harmful to claim a new finding is true when it is not, compared to missing a true finding.</edge>
</known_edges>
<missing_nodes>
  <node id="V_target1">Reasons why economics is considered a social science.</node>
  <node id="V_target2">Explanation of why Type II errors are more concerning in social science.</node>
</missing_nodes>
<missing_edges>
  <edge id="E_target1" source="V1" target="V_target1">Mechanism or rationale for classifying economics as a social science.</edge>
  <edge id="E_target2" source="V3" target="V_target2">Logical reasoning for the preference of avoiding Type II errors in social science.</edge>
</missing_edges>
topology_reasoning: The problem can be answered by a single, unified logical graph. The known nodes and edges provide a clear foundation for understanding the relationship between social science and economics, as well as the preference for avoiding Type II errors. The missing nodes and edges are specific and can be logically connected to the known entities. Therefore, the structure is best represented by a [Single Graph] topology.

query:
The Canonical New-Keynesian Model

I'm trying to learn about new-keynesian models hw they were derived. However, I found great difficulty in deriving the equations used in many studies and the lack of books and papers that mention their origin. The following equation (see link slide 7/26, https://slideplayer.com/slide/10639387/) represents the aggregate demand block of the canonical gap model (reduced-form new-keynesian model) which is a s follows,

𝑦̂ 𝑡=𝑎1𝑦̂ 𝑡−1−𝑎2𝑚𝑐𝑖𝑡+𝑎3𝑦̂ 𝑡+1+𝜖𝑦𝑡,
𝑚𝑐𝑖𝑡=𝑎4𝑧̂ 𝑡+(1−𝑎4)𝑟̂ 𝑡.

Where 𝑦̂ 𝑡
 is the output gap, 𝑚𝑐𝑖𝑡
 represents the monetary conditions index, 𝑟̂ 𝑡
 refers to the real interest rate gap, 𝑧̂ 𝑡
 denotes the real exchange rate gap. Can anyone please show how such equation was derived or direct me to its source. Thanks in advance.

The core question is about the logical and mathematical steps that lead to the formulation of these equations, as well as references to the original sources or detailed derivations. The foundational cognitive framework required here is a deep understanding of macroeconomic theory, particularly the New-Keynesian school, and the ability to trace the development of economic models through academic literature.

query:
Quadratic Form Single Summation notation

I'm trying to translate what feels like a double summation notation , compacted into a single summation. It's the summation definition of quadratic forms.

𝑄(𝑥1,𝑥2.....𝑥𝑛)=∑𝑖≤𝑗𝑎𝑖𝑗𝑥𝑖𝑥𝑗

I assume quadratic forms can be written as the less compact double summation?

𝑄(𝑥1,𝑥2.....𝑥𝑛)=∑𝑛𝑖=1∑𝑛𝑗=1𝑎𝑖𝑗𝑥𝑖𝑥𝑗

To count this we would:

Fix the outer summation first, so in this case we would be fixing rows and going across columns. Alternately we could switch the summation so we fixed columns and counted rows. This holds for any 𝑛•𝑚
 matrix i believe?
Question: I suppose the 𝑖≤𝑗
 notation has to correspond to this. But it's not immediately clicking?
query_intent_analysis: The user is trying to understand the relationship between a compact single summation notation and a double summation notation for quadratic forms. The core question is how the condition \(i \leq j\) in the single summation notation translates to the double summation notation, and how this affects the counting of terms in the quadratic form.

  <node id="V2">Quadratic form in double summation: \(Q(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) = \sum_{i=1}^n \sum_{j=1}^n a_{ij} x_i x_j\)</node>
</known_nodes>
<known_edges>

</known_edges>

</missing_nodes>
<missing_edges>
  <edge id="E_target1" source="V1" target="V_target1">Explanation of the logical mechanism that ensures the single summation with \(i \leq j\) is equivalent to the double summation, but without redundant terms.</edge>
</missing_edges>

The known nodes and edges provide the context of the two different notations for the quadratic form, and the missing edge explains the logical mechanism that connects these notations. The condition \(i \leq j\) in the single summation ensures that each term is counted only once, which is the key to understanding the equivalence. query:
Is there any example for "pluralistic ignorance" in economics?

Today I read a paper recently written by Bursztyn, 2021 saying that

The origin, persistence, and rigidity of misperceptions about others can in principle be explained by different conceptual frameworks, such as stereotyping (e.g., Bordalo et al. 2016), motivated reasoning (e.g., Benabou and Tirole 2016), and pluralistic ignorance (e.g., Kuran 1997; Bursztyn, Egorov and Fiorin 2020; Bursztyn, Gonz´alez and Yanagizawa-Drott 2020)

From what I understand, pluralistic ignorance is

the situation in which almost all members of a group privately reject group norms, yet believe that virtually all other group members accept them

However, I still not yet got the idea of this behaviour, could you give me an example in economic or finance to understand it more?

Update: Adding a great explaination of "pluralistic ignorance" as suggested by @Lason

The term “pluralistic ignorance” was coined to describe the situation in which almost all members of a group privately reject group norms, yet believe that virtually all other group members accept them (Katz and Allport, 1931). Under such situations, individuals predict that they would lose social standing if they behaved as they wished. Behaving against the group norm could result in negative reactions from other ingroup members. Therefore, people are likely to follow perceived group norms to maintain positive impressions in their groups, even when they do not support the norms (Miller and McFarland, 1987; Miller and Prentice, 1994; Prentice and Miller, 1996; Geiger and Swim, 2016). In line with this idea, in situations of pluralistic ignorance, some people even actively enforce the perceived norms (i.e., publicly criticizing a “misfit” into accepting the norm), although they privately disapprove of the norms (Willer et al., 2009). Consequently, public behaviors of groups as a whole do not coincide with the majority of group members' private preferences under circumstances of pluralistic ignorance. Thus, the situation of pluralistic ignorance is well represented in the following sentence: “No one believes, but everyone believes that everyone else believes” (Krech and Crutchfield, 1948).
query_intent_analysis: The user is seeking to understand the concept of "pluralistic ignorance" in the context of economics or finance. The core question is to provide a concrete example that illustrates how pluralistic ignorance can manifest in economic or financial settings. The foundational cognitive framework required here is a multi-perspective analysis, combining social psychology with economic behavior.

  <node id="V3">Individuals follow perceived group norms to maintain social standing, even if they do not support the norms.</node>

query:
Proving existence of a unique pure strategy Nash Equilibrium in two-person continuous games

I am working on a game theory exercise and I want to prove existence and uniqueness of pure strategy NE for a 2 person game with continuous strategy spaces. Lets call the payoff functions 𝜋1
 and 𝜋2
 for players 1 and 2. The strategy spaces are simple one dimensional intervals in Euclidean space that are common across players (𝑠1,𝑠2∈𝑆=[𝑎,𝑏]
). What do I need to do to show that the payoff functions are diagonally strictly concave? I am having a hard time wrapping my head around Rosen's paper and I don't need many dimensional strategy spaces and n players for now. I was wondering if there was a simpler, easy to understand version of the result for my simplified game. If someone could also provide some intuition for that condition, it would also be a massive help! Thanks

The core question is how to show that the payoff functions are diagonally strictly concave, which is a condition that ensures the existence and uniqueness of a pure strategy NE. The foundational cognitive framework required here is a deep understanding of game theory, specifically the conditions under which a unique pure strategy NE exists in continuous games.

Has someone articulated a theory of network effects as the source of monopoly?

Many thanks.

The foundational cognitive framework required here is an advanced economic and theoretical analysis, potentially involving both historical and contemporary perspectives on industrial organization.
